_note: Media + info content for the map. Images under public/landmap/media/<id>/ are PLACEHOLDER aerial renders exported from the QGIS master plan — to be replaced with Nano Banana concept art (see WellsGray_ConceptShots/PROMPTS.md). writeup supports line breaks / blank-line paragraphs. video = a YouTube/Vimeo embed URL or empty. mindmap = deep link to portal.place/mind-map.

areas.title: Wellness Village
areas.blurb: Car-free pod village around one shared commons, with a lakeside spa.
areas.writeup:
The step from seasonal stays to people living here year-round. Small clusters of timber cabins — "pods" of about six homes around a shared green — are spread across the old golf course, each woven into its own permaculture food garden rather than zoned off into a single farm.

Every path converges on The Commons: one central hub with a café & kitchen, maker space, co-working, and movement + wellness studios — so the whole village meets in one place. A Lakeside Spa (sauna, cold-plunge, soaking deck, treatment cabins) sits on the west pond.

Designed around the culture, schooling and mutual aid the community is already building.

areas.title: Maker Village
areas.blurb: Prefab cabin cluster for makers and builders.
areas.writeup:
A cluster of prefab cabins for the people who build the village. Self-sufficiency means being able to make what you need, not just buy it — so this neighbourhood pairs housing with shared maker infrastructure: workshops, power and fast internet.

The maker space here manufactures the tiny homes and cabins used across the property, built to a template that can export to other village nodes — keeping the craft, materials and money inside the local economy.

areas.title: Artist Village
areas.blurb: Cabin community for artists and residencies.
areas.writeup:
A quiet cabin community on a forested slope above the lake, built for studios, residencies and creative work — art treated as infrastructure from day one.

The cabins come in four types, shown by colour on the plan: small studio cabins, larger medium cabins, low-profile "hill" cabins tucked into the slope, and angular "cliffside" cabins built in hempcrete. Walking paths link them; a small parking area sits at the edge.

areas.title: Festival Grounds
areas.writeup:
A 35-acre festival meadow south of the lake, phased from 500 to 5,000 attendees — the gatherings and shared rituals that turn neighbours into a tribe.

Self-sufficient and leave-no-trace: organised camping bands on the grass, a winding discovery trail dotted with art "landings", central showers, grouped sanitation, and event parking at the north edge. No permanent buildings — the meadow returns to open land between events.

areas.title: Subdivision Lots
areas.blurb: Large acreage lots for sale — funding the build.
areas.writeup:
Residential acreage lots for sale that help fund the larger buildout — turning a slice of the land into the capital for everything else.

Two options are drawn on the plan (toggle them in the layers panel): uniform ~10-acre lots, or a mix of 10 / 5 / 2.5-acre lots. Each is mostly treed with a cleared building envelope and a shared gravel access road.

areas.title: Campsite (existing)
areas.writeup:
The existing, operating campground — the working prototype that's already built and earning. Full-service and power/water RV sites, rental accommodations, shower houses and the resort buildings, set among the trees beside the private lake.

This is Phase 1: proof that the land works as a place before the residential village is built on top of it.

areas.title: RV & Tiny Home Sites
areas.writeup:
A Phase 3 expansion of the campground: rows of pull-through RV pads and tiny-home sites with upgraded 50-amp power, water and septic.

The tiny homes are built on-site in the village's own maker space — the first homes manufactured to the template that later builds the residential village and exports to other nodes.

areas.title: Regenerative Farm
areas.blurb: Permaculture market farm feeding the village.
areas.writeup:
A regenerative farm feeding residents and the wider network — resilience built on food you can count on, grown at a human scale right beside where people live.

Designed to permaculture principles: long curving contour beds, a circular mandala garden and herb spiral, a food forest of mixed fruit and nut trees, an irrigation pond and swales, polytunnel greenhouses and a timber barn — all linked by mulched paths. Smaller food gardens are also woven in beside each village pod, so growing food is part of everyday life rather than something set apart.
